status: first-pass evidence bundle exported from tracker rows

observations.observation_value: Peripheral axon injury made long spinal primary sensory axons far more likely to regenerate into peripheral nerve grafts, implicating inducible neuronal events.
observations.normalized_value: peripheral injury enhances central sensory axon regeneration
observations.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

observations.observation_value: Conditioning accelerated peripheral branch regeneration but did not significantly accelerate dorsal-root central branch regeneration in this assay.
observations.statistics_reported: Peripheral branch rate increased from 4.4 to 5.5 mm/day; dorsal-root central branch not significantly affected
observations.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

observations.observation_value: Preconditioning 1-2 weeks before dorsal column injury increased central axon growth into spinal cord above the lesion.
observations.normalized_value: preconditioning enables dorsal column growth beyond lesion

observations.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

observations.observation_value: Two priming lesions sustained intrinsic growth capacity and promoted regeneration within and beyond spinal lesion after SCI.

observations.observation_value: Commentary highlights that peripheral DRG conditioning can enable central axon regeneration and that sequence/timing determines effect.
observations.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Peripheral axotomy can alter the regenerative behavior of primary sensory neurons and increase central regeneration potential.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Conditioning effects are branch- and assay-specific, so peripheral growth acceleration should not be assumed to equal central branch regeneration.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Preconditioning peripheral axotomy can induce a DRG intrinsic growth state sufficient to support dorsal column axon growth beyond a spinal lesion.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Repeated peripheral priming can sustain intrinsic growth capacity after SCI and improve regeneration when single post-injury priming is insufficient.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Peripheral conditioning lesions recruit broader and more durable genetic programs than cAMP elevation alone.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: JAK/STAT3 activation in DRG neurons is necessary for conditioning-induced growth competence and central sensory axon regeneration.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: IL-6 is required for conditioning-induced GAP-43 upregulation and dorsal column regeneration in the tested mouse model.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: cAMP signaling can mimic part of the conditioning lesion effect but should be treated as a partial mechanism.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Adult sensory neuron regeneration competence depends on transcriptional growth-state switching.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Conditioning changes multiple neurite-growth parameters, so regeneration capacity should not be represented as one undifferentiated endpoint.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Unilateral sciatic nerve injury can trigger a systemic or remote pro-regenerative DRG response outside the directly injured nerve territory.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Enriched conditioning expands sensory-neuron regenerative ability through a neuronal intrinsic PKC-STAT3-NOX2 redox signaling axis.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: DRG macrophages and inflammatory mediators help maintain conditioning-induced regenerative capacity.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Spinal glial proliferation can contribute to conditioning lesion-induced sensory axon regeneration into the injured spinal cord.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: The dorsal column model is a strong system for separating peripheral and central branch injury responses in the same DRG neuron population.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

author_claims.claim_text: Peripheral nerve/DRG conditioning is a host-priming comparator for SCI repair, but sequence, sensory-pathway specificity, and translation constraints must be retained.
author_claims.source_section: Module 15A tracker / source-page, full-text PMC, or review metadata

evidence_links.notes: Dorsal column model row supports sensory pathway boundary.
Broad genetic programs contextualize sustained effect.
Transcriptional switch row supports broad mechanism boundary.
Enriched conditioning row supports STAT3 redox axis.
STAT3 row supports IL-6/JAK pathway boundary.
Conditioning versus cAMP row supports partial mimicry boundary.
Broad gene mechanism row supports transcriptional switching.
Enriched conditioning contextualizes systemic/expanded priming logic.
DRG macrophage row contextualizes systemic/inflammatory mediator boundary.
Remote DRG row contextualizes immune-mediated systemic response.
DRG macrophage row separates ganglion versus spinal glia.
Dorsal column positive row supports host-priming comparator.
Repeated post-SCI priming contextualizes translation timing.
Broad genetic mechanism row supports comparator value.
Remote DRG row supports systemic priming boundary.

boundary_records.boundary_statement: Peripheral nerve/DRG conditioning must be separated from generic axon-regeneration interventions because the causal variable is prior or concurrent peripheral priming.
Lesion order and interval are central: preconditioning, concurrent conditioning, post-conditioning, two-lesion priming, and enriched conditioning are not interchangeable.
DRG side, level, and association with the injured peripheral nerve must be recorded, especially for contralateral or remote priming claims.
cAMP is a partial mimic of conditioning, while conditioning lesions recruit broader genetic and cellular mechanisms.
IL-6/JAK/STAT3, redox/NOX2, macrophage, and glial mechanisms should be separated rather than summarized as generic inflammation.
Conditioning effects are sensory-pathway and branch-specific; dorsal column DRG evidence should not be generalized to all CNS axon systems without direct evidence.
Peripheral nerve priming has clinical-translation constraints because deliberate pre-injury lesions are not direct therapeutic analogs.
Remote/contralateral DRG priming is a mechanistic host-state clue, not proof that contralateral SCI repair will improve unless central repair endpoints are directly tested.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Peripheral nerve injury can induce a DRG intrinsic growth state that improves central sensory axon regeneration, but the claim is strongest for DRG/dorsal-column sensory systems and must preserve peripheral lesion, central test lesion, species, and endpoint boundaries.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Peripheral nerve injury can induce a DRG intrinsic growth state that improves central sensory axon regeneration, but the claim is strongest for DRG/dorsal-column sensory systems and must preserve peripheral lesion, central test lesion, species, and endpoint boundaries.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Timing is a causal variable in conditioning-lesion biology: preconditioning, concurrent priming, post-SCI priming, repeated priming, and enriched conditioning produce different regenerative outcomes and should be analyzed as separate intervention classes.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Timing is a causal variable in conditioning-lesion biology: preconditioning, concurrent priming, post-SCI priming, repeated priming, and enriched conditioning produce different regenerative outcomes and should be analyzed as separate intervention classes.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: cAMP/PKA signaling can mimic part of the conditioning effect, but peripheral nerve conditioning recruits broader and more durable transcriptional programs, so cAMP should be treated as one mechanism rather than the whole priming state.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: cAMP/PKA signaling can mimic part of the conditioning effect, but peripheral nerve conditioning recruits broader and more durable transcriptional programs, so cAMP should be treated as one mechanism rather than the whole priming state.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: IL-6/JAK/STAT3 signaling is a consensus-ready mediator family for conditioning-induced DRG growth competence, with enriched-conditioning evidence extending this axis into PKC-STAT3-NOX2 redox signaling after SCI.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: IL-6/JAK/STAT3 signaling is a consensus-ready mediator family for conditioning-induced DRG growth competence, with enriched-conditioning evidence extending this axis into PKC-STAT3-NOX2 redox signaling after SCI.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Conditioning lesion effects include non-neuronal support: DRG macrophages and spinal glial division contribute to the maintenance or expression of regenerative capacity and should be separated by compartment and timing.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Conditioning lesion effects include non-neuronal support: DRG macrophages and spinal glial division contribute to the maintenance or expression of regenerative capacity and should be separated by compartment and timing.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Unilateral sciatic nerve injury can induce pro-regenerative marker expression and enhanced growth capacity in DRGs not directly associated with the damaged nerve, making remote or contralateral DRG priming a real boundary to track separately from ipsilateral conditioning.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Unilateral sciatic nerve injury can induce pro-regenerative marker expression and enhanced growth capacity in DRGs not directly associated with the damaged nerve, making remote or contralateral DRG priming a real boundary to track separately from ipsilateral conditioning.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Peripheral conditioning is not a universal regeneration switch: early branch-specific negative evidence and dorsal-column model boundaries show that peripheral, central, ipsilateral, contralateral, and tract-specific outcomes must remain separated.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Peripheral conditioning is not a universal regeneration switch: early branch-specific negative evidence and dorsal-column model boundaries show that peripheral, central, ipsilateral, contralateral, and tract-specific outcomes must remain separated.

consensus_drafts.draft_statement: Module 15 should function as a peripheral-to-CNS host-priming comparator for SCI repair: candidate synthesis should ask whether a treatment mimics DRG growth-state induction, sustains that state after SCI, recruits systemic/remote DRG programs, or changes the lesion environment enough for central axon growth.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Module 15 should function as a peripheral-to-CNS host-priming comparator for SCI repair: candidate synthesis should ask whether a treatment mimics DRG growth-state induction, sustains that state after SCI, recruits systemic/remote DRG programs, or changes the lesion environment enough for central axon growth.
